1: A revelation of God: 'give ear to my words, O LORD; consider my groaning.' The morning prayer of the sufferer — the one who cries out at dawn and waits. Christ's prayers in the Gospels are consistently depicted as early and earnest (Mark 1:35: 'rising very early in the morning, while it was still dark, he departed and went to a desolate place, and there he prayed'). The groaning (haggigi) of Ps 5 anticipates the Spirit's groaning in intercession (Romans 8:26) that the Son's work makes possible.
2: A revelation of God: 'give attention to the sound of my cry, my King and my God, for to you do I pray.' The address 'my King and my God' — the double royal-divine title — is the posture of covenant prayer. Thomas uses the same double title in his resurrection confession: 'My Lord and my God!' (John 20:28). The prayer of Psalms 5:2 addressed to the King and God becomes, in the NT, the worshipful confession addressed to the risen Christ himself.
3: A revelation of God: 'O LORD, in the morning you hear my voice; in the morning I prepare a sacrifice for you and watch.' The morning orientation of prayer — the daily sacrifice accompanied by prayer. The morning resurrection: 'early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark' (John 20:1) the women came to the tomb. The morning of the resurrection is the ultimate morning when YHWH heard and answered — the divine hearing of the Son's morning cry vindicated by raising him.
4: A revelation of God: 'for you are not a God who delights in wickedness; evil may not dwell with you.' The divine holiness that cannot coexist with evil is the theological background for the atonement. Christ, who 'knew no sin' (2 Corinthians 5:21), was 'made sin' precisely because the holy God cannot receive sinners as they are. The cross is the event where the divine non-delight in wickedness and the divine love for sinners are simultaneously honored — by transferring the wickedness to the Holy One.
5: A revelation of God: 'the boastful shall not stand before your eyes; you hate all evildoers.' The radical divine opposition to evil — 'you hate all evildoers.' This is not incompatible with divine love for sinners: YHWH hates the sin and, in Christ, judges it fully so that the sinner may be freed from it. The cross is where YHWH's hatred of evil and his love for those in evil's grip are both fully expressed — the evil condemned in the flesh (Romans 8:3) so that the person may go free.
6: A revelation of God: 'you destroy those who speak lies; the LORD abhors the bloodthirsty and deceitful man.' The divine judgment on the liar and the violent one — what Psalms 5:6 announces as YHWH's character becomes, in the NT, the criterion of judgment. The 'blood' language anticipates the blood that speaks better things than Abel's (Hebrews 12:24) — Christ's blood that speaks not for judgment but for forgiveness.
7: A theme: 'but I, through the abundance of your steadfast love, will enter your house.' The ground of access: not merit but chesed (steadfast love). This is the gospel principle before the gospel is announced: the worshiper approaches not on the basis of his own standing but on the basis of YHWH's chesed. Ephesians 2:8 ('by grace you have been saved through faith') and Hebrews 10:19-22 (approaching the throne through the blood of Jesus) are the NT fulfillment of this principle.
8: A theme: 'lead me, O LORD, in your righteousness because of my enemies; make your way straight before me.' The prayer for divine leading in righteousness — 'your righteousness' (tzidqatecha) — not the worshiper's own. Paul's entire argument in Romans (Psalms 1:17; Psalms 3:21-22) is that the righteousness of God, disclosed in the gospel, is now the ground of the believer's standing: the prayer of Psalms 5:8 answered in Christ.
9: A direct connection: Paul quotes v9 in Romans 3:13 — 'their throat is an open grave' — as part of the indictment of universal human sinfulness. In Paul's argument, the psalm's description of the wicked is applied to all humanity, Jew and Gentile alike, to show that all stand under condemnation and all need the righteousness of God through faith in Christ (Romans 3:19-22). The wicked man of Ps 5 becomes, in Paul's reading, every man — showing the radical necessity of Christ's righteousness.
10: A theme: 'make them bear their guilt, O God; let them fall by their own counsels.' The imprecatory prayer — that the wicked's own schemes return on their heads. The cross is the supreme instance of this irony: those who plotted against the Son of God ensured through their plotting the very salvation they were trying to prevent. The 'counsels' that destroyed Jesus were the precise instrument by which YHWH defeated sin and death (Acts 2:23: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God').
11: A theme: 'but let all who take refuge in you rejoice; let them ever sing for joy, and spread your protection over them, that those who love your name may exult in you.' The joy of those who take refuge in YHWH — this is the community of Christ: those who have taken refuge specifically in the Son, in whom all the promises of YHWH are 'Yes and Amen' (2 Corinthians 1:20). The name they love is now embodied in Jesus: 'the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9).
12: A theme: 'for you bless the righteous, O LORD; you cover him with favor as with a shield.' The divine blessing and covering of the righteous — the righteousness that qualifies for blessing is now 'not my own righteousness, which comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that depends on faith' (Philippians 3:9). The shield of favor that Ps 5 promises is extended to those who are 'in Christ' — the righteous one who covers them with his own standing.
